You are an expert researcher, strategic analyzer and software engineer with a deep understanding of experimental design and reproducibility in scientific research.
You will receive a research paper in JSON format, an overview of the plan, a design in JSON format consisting of "Implementation approach", "File list", "Data structures and interfaces", and "Program call flow", followed by a task in JSON format that includes "Required packages", "Required other language third-party packages", "Logic Analysis", and "Task list", along with a configuration file named "config.yaml". 

Your task is to conduct a comprehensive logic analysis to accurately reproduce the experiments and methodologies described in the research paper. 
This analysis must align precisely with the paper’s methodology, experimental setup, and evaluation criteria.

1. Align with the Paper: Your analysis must strictly follow the methods, datasets, model configurations, hyperparameters, and experimental setups described in the paper.
2. Be Clear and Structured: Present your analysis in a logical, well-organized, and actionable format that is easy to follow and implement.
3. Prioritize Efficiency: Optimize the analysis for clarity and practical implementation while ensuring fidelity to the original experiments.
4. Follow design: YOU MUST FOLLOW "Data structures and interfaces". DONT CHANGE ANY DESIGN. Do not use public member functions that do not exist in your design.
5. REFER TO CONFIGURATION: Always reference settings from the config.yaml file. Do not invent or assume any values—only use configurations explicitly provided.

## Paper
{'paper_id': '', 'title': 'Geolocation of the Ionospheric Irregularities in the Equatorial F Layer by Back Propagation of COSMIC-2 Radio Occultation Signals', 'abstract': "Plasma irregularities in the ionosphere induce scintillation of radio signals. Radio occultation (RO) observations of the Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) signals from low Earth orbit (LEO) allow monitoring of the ionospheric scintillation. Under certain conditions, it is possible to localize (geolocate) plasma irregularities along the line-of-sight between the GNSS and LEO satellites. While several techniques have been considered for the localization, in this study we use the back propagation (BP) of complex RO signals (phase and amplitude) measured at a high rate (HR), 50-100 Hz. Our method is based on a numerical solution of the wave equation, originally proposed for geolocation in 2002, with some modifications. We consider theoretical aspects of the BP technique, including assumptions, approximations and limitations, and perform numerical modeling of radio wave propagation. We investigate geolocation by BP for two regions with aligned and mis-aligned irregularities and explain multi-valued geolocations. We focus on the equatorial F region, consistent with the COSMIC-2 observation sampling and use the IGRF-13 model of the Earth's magnetic field to define the orientation of plasma irregularities. We use our method for processing of COSMIC-2 HR scintillation data collected from the precise orbit determination antennas for 2 years: 2021 and 2023 (years with low and high solar activity). The results, represented by gridded monthly maps of geolocations, show clear seasonal and interannual variations. Additionally, we present comparison of the geolocations obtained independently from L1 and L2 signals for a 2-month period.", 'pdf_parse': {'paper_id': '', 'abstract': [{'text': "Plasma irregularities in the ionosphere induce scintillation of radio signals. Radio occultation (RO) observations of the Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) signals from low Earth orbit (LEO) allow monitoring of the ionospheric scintillation. Under certain conditions, it is possible to localize (geolocate) plasma irregularities along the line-of-sight between the GNSS and LEO satellites. While several techniques have been considered for the localization, in this study we use the back propagation (BP) of complex RO signals (phase and amplitude) measured at a high rate (HR), 50-100 Hz. Our method is based on a numerical solution of the wave equation, originally proposed for geolocation in 2002, with some modifications. We consider theoretical aspects of the BP technique, including assumptions, approximations and limitations, and perform numerical modeling of radio wave propagation. We investigate geolocation by BP for two regions with aligned and mis-aligned irregularities and explain multi-valued geolocations. We focus on the equatorial F region, consistent with the COSMIC-2 observation sampling and use the IGRF-13 model of the Earth's magnetic field to define the orientation of plasma irregularities. We use our method for processing of COSMIC-2 HR scintillation data collected from the precise orbit determination antennas for 2 years: 2021 and 2023 (years with low and high solar activity). The results, represented by gridded monthly maps of geolocations, show clear seasonal and interannual variations. Additionally, we present comparison of the geolocations obtained independently from L1 and L2 signals for a 2-month period.", 'section': 'Abstract', 'sec_num': None}], 'body_text': [{'text': 'The Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) radio occultation (RO) technique has been used for remote sensing of the neutral atmosphere and ionosphere since 1995 (Hajj & Romans, 1998; Kursinski et al., 1997; Rocken et al., 1997; Schreiner et al., 1999) . Irregularities of electron density in the ionosphere, with scales of about 1 km and larger, cause scintillation of radio signals. Ionospheric scintillation introduces additional noise which degrades the neutral atmosphere profiling (Kursinski et al., 1997; Ludwig-Barbosa et al., 2020; Rocken et al., 1997; Sokolovskiy et al., 2009) . Also, scintillation disrupts radio communications and navigation (Doherty et al., 2003; de Paula et al., 2020; Ishii et al., 2024; Kintner et al., 2007) . The strongest amplitude and phase scintillations occur primarily in the equatorial region where they are induced by the small-scale irregularities embedded in the post-sunset plasma depletions (bubbles) (Kelley, 1989; Tsunoda et al., 1982) . The existence of scintillation indicates the existence of the ionospheric irregularities along the line of sight (LOS) between the GNSS transmitter (Tx) and the receiver (Rx) in low Earth orbit (LEO). However, the location of irregularities along the LOS, generally, is not known. This is shown in Figure 1 . In a most simple approach, the region with irregularities is assigned to the LOS tangent point (Dymond, 2012) . However, under certain conditions, it is possible to localize irregularities more accurately along the LOS. This is useful for studies of the morphology of irregularities. Also, localization of irregularities along the GNSS-LEO LOS may help to predict scintillation on other radio links. Various approaches have been proposed for localization (geolocation) of the ionospheric irregularities (Carrano et al., 2017 (Carrano et al., , 2019)) . In this study, we use the back propagation (BP) method (Carrano et al., 2019; Groves & Carrano, 2022; Ludwig-Barbosa et al., 2023; Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) , which requires HR 50-100 Hz sampling of the complex RO signal (phase and amplitude), to capture the spectrum of scintillation. Geolocation by BP is based on solving the wave equation in a vacuum by using the phase and amplitude, measured on the Rx trajectory, as the boundary condition.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'Localization by BP relies on several assumptions and approximations. First, plasma irregularities must occupy a limited volume so that wave propagation may be approximately considered using a thin phase screen (PS) approximation (Wernik et al., 2007) . In other words, the phase fluctuations, which develop inside the volume with irregularities, are assigned to the PS while the amplitude fluctuations are considered in the free space after propagation through the PS, where they increase with the distance from the PS to Rx due to focusing/defocusing effects. The amplitude fluctuations of BP radio waves decrease from the Rx to the PS and then increase again from the PS to Tx. Thus, the location of the PS (the volume with irregularities) is estimated from the minimum of the BP amplitude fluctuation. Second, plasma irregularities must be elongated or anisotropic with a significant coefficient of anisotropy. This allows reduction of the three-dimensional (3D) BP problem to the two-dimensional (2D) one. Otherwise, the BP problem is under-determined because the phase and amplitude are measured on a one-dimensional (1D) Rx trajectory. Formal application of the 2D BP does not allow localization of the region with 3D isotropic irregularities (Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) because the transverse propagation effects are not accounted for. Third, the orientation of the irregularities should be known. This allows one to define the plane perpendicular to projection of the irregularities on the PS, project Tx and Rx trajectories onto that plane and solve the 2D wave propagation problem in that plane (hereafter called the BP plane). In this study we focus on the equatorial F region where plasma irregularities inside plasma bubbles commonly have sufficient anisotropy and are aligned with magnetic field lines (Carrano et al., 2011) , both key assumptions of the BP localization technique. This requires the model of magnetic field; in this study we use the IGRF-13 model (Alken et al., 2021) ; links to publicly available source codes and data are available in this paper. Because the location of irregularities along the LOS is not known in advance, the BP is applied for different orientations of the BP plane corresponding to different distances along the LOS where the magnetic field vector is used to define BP plane. This results in different distances from Rx to the minimum of BP amplitude fluctuation. When the distance to the point where the magnetic field model is used to define the BP plane equals the distance to the minimum of BP amplitude fluctuation found in that plane, this distance is considered the distance to irregularities. Yet another limitation of the BP localization technique, related to the orientation of Rx orbit and the structure of magnetic field along the LOS, is discussed in Section 4. 2 of 21 BP has been applied for the GNSS RO signals received in LEO in some previous studies. Mortensen and Hoeg (1998) , Gorbunov and Gurvich (1998) , Ao et al. (2003) applied BP for reduction of the diffraction and multipath effects on RO signals in the troposphere. Sokolovskiy et al. (2013) applied BP for reduction of the errors of ionospheric correction in the stratosphere. Gorbunov et al. (2002) , Cherniak et al. (2019) , Ludwig-Barbosa et al. (2023) applied BP for localization of plasma irregularities in the ionosphere (in the Es and F layers), by explicitly or implicitly assuming their horizontal orientation. In previous studies (Carrano et al., 2019; Groves & Carrano, 2022; Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) and in this study, BP is applied for localization of plasma irregularities in the F layer without an assumption of their horizontal orientation, by modeling the magnetic field instead (results of this study confirm that accounting for the orientation of plasma irregularities is important). Following (Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) , the distance to irregularities is found by BP which is applied for the RO signal in coordinate representation. Carrano et al. (2019) , Groves and Carrano (2022) apply BP for RO signal in time representation by finding the Fresnel frequency corresponding to the region with irregularities. The Fresnel frequency is then converted to distance by using relation to Fresnel radius and scan velocity as the function of the distance along the LOS. We note that Carrano et al. (2017 Carrano et al. ( , 2019 ) also considered other geolocation methods which use different estimates of the Fresnel frequency, but the same conversion to the distance using scan velocity.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'In this study, we apply BP for COSMIC-2 (Constellation Observing System for Meteorology, Ionosphere and Climate) HR phase and amplitude data. COSMIC-2 is a constellation of six low-inclination (24 deg) LEO satellites (orbit height ∼550 km) with GNSS receivers and antennas designed for RO sensing of the neutral atmosphere and ionosphere in low to middle latitudes, and is well suitable for monitoring of the equatorial scintillation (Schreiner et al., 2020; Weiss et al., 2022) .', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'The paper is structured as follows. Section 1 introduces the problem. Before describing our geolocation algorithm, the COSMIC-2 scintillation data, and the results of data processing, we perform numerical modeling of the forward and back radio wave propagation. Section 2 describes the goals of this modeling and details of the forward propagation (FP) and BP for different scenarios which include geolocation in the presence of two regions with aligned and mis-aligned irregularities. Section 3 describes specific details of BP for real observational geometry including reduction of the problem from 3D to 2D, approximation of the stationary transmitter and correction of the curvature of wave front. Section 4 describes the COSMIC-2 HR scintillation data and the BP algorithm applied for geolocation of plasma irregularities at the COSMIC Data Analysis and Archive Center (CDAAC). Section 4 also includes examples of geolocations and provides an explanation of the multi-valued solutions. Section 5 presents monthly distributions of plasma irregularities in the equatorial F-layer obtained by the geolocation algorithm for two separate years, 2021 and 2023 (representing low and high solar activity) and compares geolocations obtained by processing L1 and L2 scintillation data during the 2-month test period. Section 6 discusses and summarizes the results.', 'section': 'Introduction', 'sec_num': '1.'}, {'text': 'In this section, we model FP and BP to investigate the following effects: (a) estimation of the geolocation error related to the error of assumed orientation of irregularities; (b) modeling of geolocation in the case of two regions with the same orientation of irregularities; (c) modeling of geolocation in the case of two regions with different orientations of irregularities. For modeling of the ionospheric scintillation, a region with irregularities may be represented by multiple PSs (Carrano et al., 2011; Ludwig-Barbosa et al., 2020 , 2023; Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) or by a single PS (Carrano et al., 2012a (Carrano et al., , 2012b;; Rino et al., 2019; Sokolovskiy et al., 2002) . We use the approximation of a single PS and an incident plane wave, which are sufficient for investigation of the effects in this section. As was mentioned earlier, the BP which uses observations on 1D trajectory is always 2D. However, the FP may be 2D or 3D depending on the investigated effect. For the effects (1, 2) the 2D FP is sufficient. For the effect (3) the 3D FP is applied.', 'section': 'Numerical Modeling of the Forward and Back Wave Propagation', 'sec_num': '2.'}, {'text': 'Figure 2 shows layout of the FP and BP in the case of PS with 1D irregularities. In this case, the wave field does not depend on y and it is sufficient to use the 2D FP in the xz plane. The wave field which would be measured on the Rx trajectory zʹ is projected on z = zʹ cos α, where α is the scan angle. In this section, we assume that Rx trajectory is a straight line perpendicular to wave propagation direction, which is sufficient for investigation of the effects mentioned above. Thus, the BP is applied in the xz plane perpendicular to irregularities on the PS.', 'section': 'Numerical Modeling of the Forward and Back Wave Propagation', 'sec_num': '2.'}, {'text': '10.1029/2024RS008081', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'We use the solution of the wave equation in a vacuum by expansion in a series of plane waves (this solution neglects back scattered waves and is equivalent to solving the parabolic wave equation (Knepp, 1983) ). The complex amplitude of an incident 2D plane wave is: u(x, z) = A 0 exp(ikx) where k = 2π/λ is the wave number, λ is the wavelength, and A 0 is the amplitude. The PS at x = x 0 introduces the phase delay/advance S 0 (z) . The complex amplitude on output of the PS u out (z) = A 0 exp[ikx 0 + ikS 0 (z) ] is used as the boundary condition and expanded in the Fourier series: u out (z) = ∑ m c m exp ik zm z) , where m is the number of harmonic and c m are Fourier coefficients. Each harmonic is associated with the 2D plane wave with the wave number', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'k = ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ k 2 zm + k 2 xm √', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': '. The plane waves are summated at another distance x where the solution is:', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'u(x, z) = A(x, z) exp [ikS(x, z)] = ∑ m c m exp[ik zm z + i ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ k 2 k 2 zm √ (x x 0 )] (1)', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'The same solution is applied for BP, by using u(z) on the BP trajectory x = x BP as the boundary condition and replacing x x 0 by x x BP .', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'We use the 1D model of random phase irregularities on the PS, S 0 (z) , with the 1D power spectrum (Rino, 1979) :', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Φ k z ) ∼ k 2 0 + k 2 z ) p/2 (2)', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'where p is the spectral power index (which characterizes the slope of the spectrum in logarithm representation when k z ≫ k 0 ) and k 0 = 2π/ l 0 is the wavenumber corresponding to the outer scale l 0 of random irregularities. Both parameters, k 0 and p, can be estimated by FP modeling and comparing results to observational data by using different metrics. Figure 3a shows power spectra of the intensity fluctuations modeled for p = 1, 1.5, 2, by using l 0 = 10 km and the distance between PS and Rx L = 1, 900 km (the latter two numbers are explained below).', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Red lines show approximations of the spectral slopes which are approximately equal to 2p. For p = 1.5, the spectral slope 3.05 is consistent with the value ∼3.1 obtained from scintillation data by Carrano et al. (2011) . In that study, they used outer scale l 0 = 10 km obtained from in situ measurements (Basu et al., 1983) . However, l 0 also can be obtained from the scintillation observations alone using the fact that for a given magnitude of the phase irregularities, the larger is their scale the smaller is their effect on amplitude. The outer scale affects the shape of the spectrum (2) that is, distribution of the phase irregularities with different scales, thus affecting the magnitude of the amplitude fluctuations for a given magnitude of the phase fluctuations. As the metric, we use the ratio of the phase and intensity scintillation indices, ', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'σ ϕ = k <S 2 > <S> 2 ) 1/ 2 and S 4 = (<A 4 > <A 2 > 2 ) 1/ 2 / < A 2 >. It', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': '10.1029/2024RS008081', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'is important that for a given l 0 and distance L from PS to Rx, the σ ϕ / S 4 does not depend on σ ϕ . However, the σ ϕ / S 4 substantially depends on l 0 and on L. This follows from Figure 2b which shows σ ϕ / S 4 as functions of l 0 for different L (indicated in the figure) obtained by FP by using the spectrum (2). From statistical analysis of COSMIC-2 scintillation and geolocation data used in Section 5, it follows: <σ ϕ > ≅ 1.65 rad, <S 4 > ≅ 0.32, <σ ϕ / S 4 > ≅ 5.25 rad, <L > ≅ 1, 900 km. As seen from Figure 2b , this provides an estimate of the outer scale <l 0 > ≅ 9.7 km. This result, obtained from the scintillation data alone, is close to that obtained from in situ measurements (Basu et al., 1983) . In this study, for the FP, we will use the spectrum (2) with p = 1.5 and l 0 = 10 km.', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Figure 4a , by using gray scale, shows the amplitude of the FP wave (from Tx to Rx) as the function of distance L = x x 0 from the PS with random phase S 0 (z) located at x 0 = 0. It is seen that amplitude fluctuations develop after propagation through the PS and increase with the distance L due to random focusing/defocusing. Figure 4b shows amplitude of the BP wave (from Rx to Tx). It is seen that amplitude fluctuations first decrease, have minimum at the location of the PS and then increase again. Figure 4c shows normalized BP amplitude variance V = < A 2 > / <A> 2 1 as the function of distance L. Location of the PS corresponds to the minimum of V. The function V(L) is the main function used for geolocation.', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'For 2D radio waves, projecting the signal from the observation trajectory to the BP plane is equivalent to scaling zʹ coordinate by a factor cos(α) , that is, z = zʹ cos(α), as this is shown in Figure 2 . An error ∆α is equivalent to fractional error of the scaling factor tan(α) ∆ α which is illustrated in Figure 5 (left panel). Smaller or larger scaling factor results in finding the minimum of V(L) closer to or farther away from Rx compared to true location of the PS as shown in Figure 5 (right panel). Also, for a given fractional error of the scaling factor, the geolocation error increases with the distance L from Rx to irregularities; this also is shown in Figure 5 (right panel). Numerical modeling of FP and BP shows that for a given error ∆α, the geolocation error ∆L can be approximated by the linear function of distance L (consistent with scattering of radio waves at small angles):', 'section': 'Geolocation Error Related to the Error of Anticipated Orientation of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.1.'}, {'text': '∆L = 2 L tan(α)∆α', 'section': 'Geolocation Error Related to the Error of Anticipated Orientation of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.1.'}, {'text': '(3) ', 'section': 'Geolocation Error Related to the Error of Anticipated Orientation of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.1.'}, {'text': '10.1029/2024RS008081', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'According to Matteo and Morton (2011) , a magnetic field model such as IGRF is accurate to about 1% more than 90% of the time. Table 1 shows examples of geolocation error ∆L for different α and L by using fractional model error 1% as the proxy for angular error of magnetic field vector ∆α ∼ 0.6°. The goal of this table is not to provide accurate numbers for ∆L (those numbers, obtained from the modeling, represent lower estimates), but to demonstrate the substantial dependency of ∆L on α and L, which are different for different geolocations. Thus, ∆L can be estimated individually for each geolocation. This also implies that there is one region with irregularities. Here we model geolocation for two regions, each represented by PS with the same orientation of irregularities. Such modeling was done by Ludwig-Barbosa et al. ( 2023) and our goal is comparison and verification.', 'section': 'Modeling of Geolocation for Two Phase Screens With the Same Orientation of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.2.'}, {'text': 'Figure 6 shows layout of the FP and BP for 2 PSs. Figure 7a shows functions V(L) for the separation distance 1,000 km between the PSs (similar results are obtained for other separation distances). The magnitude of phase fluctuation σ ϕ is the same for both PSs. Lines 1 and 2 show V(L) for only one of the PSs, either 1 or 2. Line 3 shows V(L) for both PSs 1 and 2. It is seen that locations of the 2 PSs cannot be resolved by BP. Figure 7b shows V(L) when both PSs are present but σ ϕ for one of the PSs (1 or 2) is 1.5 times larger than for another PS. It is seen that for the PSs with different σ ϕ , only the PS with larger σ ϕ can be localized by BP regardless of whether it is closer to or farther away from Rx, while another one cannot be localized. Overall, this result is consistent with that obtained earlier by Ludwig-Barbosa et al. (2023) . In our modeling we also see subtle increase of V(L) between PSs 1 and 2 (Figure 7a , line 3) and subtle difference of the minima 1 and 2 with the shift of minimum 1 toward 2, similar to those described by Ludwig-Barbosa et al. ( 2023), but we consider these effects insignificant as practically they most likely will be overshadowed by the difference of σ ϕ on the PSs and other unmodelled effects.', 'section': 'Modeling of Geolocation for Two Phase Screens With the Same Orientation of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.2.'}, {'text': 'Modeling of geolocation for different orientations of irregularities in different regions, to our knowledge, has not previously been considered. The goal of this modeling is to understand whether different orientations may allow localizing one of the regions when σ ϕ are equal or close for both regions. This modeling requires 3D FP which is performed similarly to the 2D FP (1), by expansion of the complex amplitude of radio waves in a series of plane waves in space. The complex amplitude after propagation of the incident plane wave A 0 exp (ikx) through the 2D PS with the phase S 0 ( y, z) is: ', 'section': 'Modeling of Geolocation for Two Phase Screens With Different Orientations of Irregularities', 'sec_num': '2.3.'}, {'text': '10.1029/2024RS008081 u(x, y, z) = A(x, y, z) exp[ikS(x, y, z)] = = ∑ mn c mn exp[ik zm z + ik yn y + i ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ ̅ k 2 k 2 zm k 2 yn √ (x x 0 )] (4)', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Figure 8 shows the layout of FP and BP for 2 PSs with different orientations of the phase irregularities; the σ ϕ are set equal for both PSs. In this modeling, we set z as the Rx trajectory and use the 2D spectrum of S 0 ( y, z) :', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Φ k y , k z ) ∼ [k 2 0 + k y cos α + k z sin α) 2 ] ν δ k z cos α k y sin α) (5)', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'where δ(k) is the delta function. Figure 9 , by using gray scale, shows the 2D amplitude A( y, z) on the observational plane after propagation through only 1st (a), only 2nd (b) and through both PSs (c). It is important that after propagation through both PSs, the radio wave field becomes 2D on an observational plane. This means that ', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': '10.1029/2024RS008081 projection of the signal from Rx trajectory on the BP plane, which implies the 1D radio wave field, formally does not have physical meaning (as it was mentioned earlier in Section 1). However, such a projection has to be applied in real processing which is based on the assumption of a single region (PS) with elongated irregularities.', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'In our testing, we set different angles α 1 and α 2 between the Rx trajectory and normal directions to irregularities on PSs 1 and 2. Next, we apply 3D FP and use the 2D signal, originally modeled on ( y, z) plane, along z axis (Rx trajectory) only. Then we project this 1D signal on z 1 and z 2 directions and apply BP. Figure 10a shows functions V(L) for α 1 = 30°(red) and α 2 = 60°(blue). It is seen that only PS 1 is localized correctly. Figure 10b shows functions V(L) when the PSs 1 and 2 are swapped, that is, α 1 = 60°(red) and α 2 = 30°(blue). In this case only PS 2 is localized correctly. Examples in Figure 10 and results of a more detailed modeling (not shown) confirm that out the 2 PSs with the same σ ϕ but different angles α 1 and α 2 only the PS with smaller α can be localized correctly, regardless of whether it is closer to or farther away from Rx. Naturally, the possibility to correctly localize the PS with smaller α reduces with the reduction of the difference between α 1 and α 2 (vanishes when α 1 = α 2 , i.e. for aligned irregularities considered in Section 2a). Thus, the angle α for 2 PSs with mis-aligned irregularities plays the role similar to σ ϕ for the 2 PSs with aligned irregularities. In the first case, only the PS with larger σ ϕ is localizable by BP while in the second case only the PS with smaller α is localizable by BP. An important difference is that for a single PS or 2 PSs with aligned 1D irregularities localization by BP formally does not depend on the orientation of Rx trajectory, while for the PSs with mis-aligned irregularities it does depend. Naturally, real plasma irregularities in multiple regions may have different σ ϕ and different α and one or another factor may be dominant for localization. Here we investigate the effect of receiver thermal noise on the shape of the function V(L) for the signal to noise ratios (SNRs) specific to the observations used in this study discussed in Section 4. The average SNR for the COSMIC-2 scintillation observations is about 400 V/V, and more than 90% of the geolocations are observed with the SNRs between 200 and 600 V/V. We note that the SNR provided by receiver is defined in the 1-Hz band and for higher-rate observations it is lower by a factor of 1/ ̅̅̅ f s √ where f s is the sampling rate in Hz. Thermal noise is the additive noise on a complex signal u = u 1 + iu 2 = A exp(iϕ) where u 1 and u 2 are in-phase and quadrature components,', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'A = ̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅̅ u 2 1 + u 2 2 √ is', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'the amplitude and ϕ = arctan(u 2 / u 1 ) is the phase (in radians). For the open loop tracking, SNR = σ u1 /A = σ u2 /A where σ u1 and σ u2 are standard deviations of the u 1 and u 2 . From this definition, it can be readily obtained σ ϕ = 1/ SNR (in radians). For example, for the 1-Hz SNR = 400 V/V, the effect of thermal noise on the phase of the signal sampled at f s = 100 Hz corresponds to σ ϕ = 1/ 40 = 0.025 rad. Next, we need a relation between sampling in time and in spatial coordinate which only is used in the modeling. It is established by', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Rx velocity projected on the BP plane, that is, dz/dt = (dz ʹ/dt ) cos α which theoretically, for the observations from LEO, can be anywhere between zero and 7 km/s, but commonly is between 2 and 4 km/s. For our estimates, we assume dz/dt = 4 km/s and f s = 100 Hz which results in 40 m sampling intervals. In our modeling we use 40-km interval in z (windowed to ∼35 km to eliminate the end effects) and 10 m steps. Thus, we further increase the standard deviations of random noises added to u 1 and u 2 by a factor of 2.', 'section': 'Radio Science', 'sec_num': None}, {'text': 'Figure 11 shows results of our modeling of the effect of noise on the function V(L) for the PS at 2,000 km from Rx which is close to average geolocation distance observed in the processing (introduced earlier in this section). We use the two values of σ ϕ . Panel A shows functions V(L) for σ ϕ = 1.65 rad which is the average σ ϕ observed in the geolocation processing (introduced earlier in this section). ## Overview of the plan
# Reproduction Plan for “Geolocation of the Ionospheric Irregularities… by Back Propagation of COSMIC-2 RO Signals”

This plan is organized as a roadmap for implementing the paper’s method from scratch. It separates:  
1. methodology to reproduce,  
2. numerical modeling experiments,  
3. COSMIC-2 observational processing,  
4. quality control and evaluation,  
5. outputs and validation targets, and  
6. unclear details requiring decisions or investigation.

---

## 1. Overall Goal

Reproduce the paper’s back-propagation geolocation method for ionospheric F-region plasma irregularities using COSMIC-2 high-rate GNSS radio occultation scintillation data.

The reproduction should generate:

1. Synthetic forward/back propagation modeling results:
   - Single phase screen localization.
   - Sensitivity to scan-angle/orientation error.
   - Two phase screens with aligned irregularities.
   - Two phase screens with misaligned irregularities.
   - Noise sensitivity.
   - Multi-valued geolocation behavior.

2. Real-data COSMIC-2 geolocation products:
   - 10-s interval geolocation estimates from HR phase/amplitude data.
   - Monthly 3° × 3° latitude/longitude maps for 2021 and 2023 using L1.
   - L1/L2 comparison for the test period specified in the paper.
   - QC statistics including accepted/rejected/multi-valued cases.

---

## 2. Core Physical and Algorithmic Assumptions

The method relies on several assumptions that must be preserved in the implementation.

### 2.1 Thin Phase Screen Approximation

The irregularity region is approximated as a thin phase screen.

- Phase fluctuations are assigned to the phase screen.
- Amplitude scintillation develops by propagation after the wave passes through the screen.
- During back propagation from receiver toward transmitter, amplitude variance decreases until the phase screen location, then increases again.
- Therefore, the irregularity location is inferred from the minimum of the back-propagated amplitude variance.

### 2.2 Anisotropic Field-Aligned Irregularities

The method assumes plasma irregularities are elongated and anisotropic.

- This permits reducing the problem from 3D to 2D.
- The irregularities are assumed aligned with the geomagnetic field.
- The IGRF-13 magnetic field model is used to determine the irregularity orientation.

### 2.3 2D Back Propagation Plane

For each candidate distance along the GNSS–LEO line of sight, define a 2D back-propagation plane.

- The plane contains the GNSS–LEO line of sight.
- It is perpendicular to the projection of the magnetic field onto the phase-screen plane.
- The COSMIC-2 observed 1D trajectory is projected into this plane.

### 2.4 Geolocation Criterion

The correct geolocation distance is found when the distance used to define the magnetic-field orientation equals the distance returned by the back-propagation amplitude-variance minimum.

Define:

\[
D(L_{\mathrm{mf}}) = L_0 - L_{\mathrm{mf}}
\]

where:

- \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\): candidate distance along LOS where IGRF is evaluated to define the BP plane.
- \(L_0\): distance of the global local minimum of the back-propagated amplitude variance \(V(L)\).

The geolocation is where:

\[
D(L_{\mathrm{mf}}) = 0
\]

---

## 3. Required Datasets

### 3.1 COSMIC-2 High-Rate Scintillation Data

Source:

- UCAR COSMIC Data Analysis and Archive Center.
- DOI: `https://doi.org/10.5065/T353-C093`

Required data products:

- High-rate phase and amplitude from COSMIC-2 POD antennas.
- Receiver orbit data.
- Metadata identifying:
  - GNSS constellation.
  - Signal type: L1, L2C, L2P, etc.
  - POD antenna direction: velocity / anti-velocity.
  - Sampling rate.
  - Time stamps.
  - SNR amplitude.
  - Carrier phase.

Important details from the paper:

- COSMIC-2 has six low-inclination LEO satellites.
- Orbit inclination: approximately 24°.
- Orbit height: approximately 520–550 km.
- HR data are collected from POD antennas.
- GPS HR sampling: 50 Hz.
- GLONASS HR sampling: 100 Hz.
- GPS signals are tracked in closed loop.
- GLONASS signals are tracked in open loop and require ground removal of data modulation and phase connection.
- Amplitude is represented by SNR scaled to a 1-Hz band.

### 3.2 GNSS Orbits and Clocks

Source:

- International GNSS Service.

Required for:

Source:

Required for:

- Projection to BP plane.

Source:

- IGRF-13 links in Alken et al. 2021.

Required for:

Source:

Required for reproducing Figure 22-style solar activity comparison.

## 4. Preprocessing of COSMIC-2 Observations

### 4.1 Read and Organize Occultation Events

For each COSMIC-2 occultation/scintillation event:

1. Load high-rate phase and amplitude/SNR.
2. Load receiver position and velocity.
3. Load transmitter position and velocity.
4. Identify signal frequency:
   - L1.
   - L2C.
   - Exclude GPS L2P for the L1/L2 comparison, because the paper excludes L2P due to semi-codeless tracking instability.
5. Segment the record into 10-s intervals.

### 4.2 Determine Sampling Rate

Use metadata or infer from timestamps.

Expected:

- GPS: 50 Hz.
- GLONASS: 100 Hz.

Each 10-s interval therefore contains approximately:

- 500 samples for GPS.
- 1000 samples for GLONASS.

### 4.3 Phase Connection and Data Modulation Removal

The paper states:

- GPS phase is already connected onboard in closed-loop tracking.
- GLONASS open-loop phase requires ground processing:
  - remove data modulation,
  - connect/unwind phase.

If COSMIC data product already provides connected phase, use it. Otherwise implement:

1. Remove navigation bit/data modulation.
2. Unwrap carrier phase.
3. Remove large-scale trend.

This is an area where exact CDAAC product details need to be verified.

### 4.4 Compute Scintillation Indices in 10-s Windows

For each 10-s interval, compute:

#### Phase Scintillation Index

\[
\sigma_\phi = \mathrm{std}(\phi_{\mathrm{detrended}})
\]

The paper uses a threshold:

\[
\sigma_\phi > 0.25 \ \mathrm{rad}
\]

Only process intervals exceeding this threshold.

#### Amplitude Scintillation Index

The paper defines amplitude through SNR and intensity-like quantities. Use:

\[
I = A^2
\]

Then compute:

\[
S_4 = \frac{\sqrt{\langle I^2\rangle - \langle I\rangle^2}}{\langle I\rangle}
\]

where \(A\) is signal amplitude, represented by SNR after suitable normalization.

The paper reports approximate statistics over accepted geolocations:

\[
\langle \sigma_\phi \rangle \approx 1.65 \ \mathrm{rad}
\]

\[
\langle S_4 \rangle \approx 0.32
\]

\[
\langle \sigma_\phi/S_4 \rangle \approx 5.25 \ \mathrm{rad}
\]

These values can be used as sanity checks.

### 4.5 Detrending

The paper shows detrended phase and uses \(\sigma_\phi\), but does not fully specify the detrending filter.

Implementation options to test:

1. Remove linear trend over each 10-s interval.
2. Remove quadratic trend over each 10-s interval.
3. Apply a high-pass filter with cutoff similar to common scintillation processing.
4. Use CDAAC-provided detrended phase if available.

For faithful reproduction, investigate CDAAC documentation. If unspecified, record the detrending choice explicitly and perform sensitivity tests.

### 4.6 Tangent-Point Height Filter

The paper processes scintillation observations where the minimum LOS tangent-point height is between:

\[
150 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

and receiver height, approximately:

\[
520 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

Before BP, require:

\[
h_{\mathrm{tan}} > 150 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

This avoids contamination from sporadic E-layer effects.

---

## 5. Numerical Modeling Experiments

Before processing real COSMIC-2 data, implement the synthetic forward/back propagation models described in Sections 2 and 4 of the paper.

These experiments are essential for validating the BP implementation.

---

# 5.1 Two-Dimensional Forward Propagation

For 2D propagation, use the plane-wave expansion solution.

Incident wave:

\[
u(x,z) = A_0 \exp(ikx)
\]

where:

\[
k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}
\]

The phase screen at \(x=x_0\) imposes random phase fluctuation:

\[
S_0(z)
\]

The output immediately after the screen is:

\[
u_{\mathrm{out}}(z) = A_0 \exp[ikx_0 + ikS_0(z)]
\]

Expand:

\[
u_{\mathrm{out}}(z) = \sum_m c_m \exp(ik_{zm} z)
\]

Then propagate:

\[
u(x,z) =
\sum_m c_m
\exp
\left[
ik_{zm}z
+
i\sqrt{k^2-k_{zm}^2}(x-x_0)
\right]
\]

This is equivalent to solving the parabolic wave equation and neglecting backscatter.

### Modeling Wavelengths

Use GNSS carrier wavelengths:

- GPS L1: approximately 0.1903 m.
- GPS L2: approximately 0.2442 m.

The paper does not explicitly state which wavelength was used in synthetic figures, so start with L1 unless testing L2 effects.

---

# 5.2 Random Phase Screen Spectrum

Generate 1D random phase irregularities using the spectrum:

\[
\Phi(k_z) \sim (k_0^2 + k_z^2)^{-p/2}
\]

where:

\[
k_0 = \frac{2\pi}{l_0}
\]

Use the paper’s parameters:

\[
p = 1.5
\]

\[
l_0 = 10 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

The paper estimates:

\[
\langle l_0 \rangle \approx 9.7 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

from observed COSMIC-2 scintillation statistics, consistent with \(l_0=10\) km.

### Synthetic Phase Screen Construction

Implementation steps:

1. Define spatial grid \(z\).
2. Generate Fourier coefficients with random phases.
3. Scale amplitudes according to \(\Phi(k_z)\).
4. Enforce Hermitian symmetry for real \(S_0(z)\).
5. Inverse FFT to obtain \(S_0(z)\).
6. Normalize to desired \(\sigma_\phi\).

The phase in radians is:

\[
\phi(z) = k S_0(z)
\]

Normalize such that:

\[
\mathrm{std}(\phi) = \sigma_\phi
\]

Use values:

- \(\sigma_\phi = 1.65\) rad for average observed scintillation.
- \(\sigma_\phi = 0.25\) rad for threshold/noise sensitivity tests.

---

# 5.3 Back Propagation in Synthetic Modeling

Back propagation uses the same propagation operator, but with the observed field at receiver as the boundary condition and propagation distance reversed.

For each candidate back-propagation distance \(L\), compute complex field \(u_{\mathrm{BP}}(L,z)\).

Then compute normalized amplitude variance:

\[
V(L) = \frac{\langle A^2\rangle}{\langle A\rangle^2} - 1
\]

where:

\[
A = |u_{\mathrm{BP}}|
\]

The phase screen location should correspond to the minimum of \(V(L)\).

---

# 5.4 Synthetic Experiment A: Single Phase Screen

Purpose:

- Verify that the minimum of \(V(L)\) occurs at the true phase screen distance.

Suggested setup:

- One 1D phase screen.
- \(p=1.5\).
- \(l_0=10\) km.
- Phase screen to receiver distance:
  - \(L=1900\) km for average observational case.
  - Also test \(L=2000\) km, used in the noise modeling.
- \(\sigma_\phi=1.65\) rad.

Expected result:

- BP amplitude fluctuations decrease toward the true screen.
- \(V(L)\) has a clear minimum at the true screen distance.

---

# 5.5 Synthetic Experiment B: Outer-Scale Estimation

Reproduce the logic of Figure 3.

For a range of outer scales \(l_0\), compute:

\[
\sigma_\phi/S_4
\]

for several screen-receiver distances \(L\).

Distances to test:

- The paper’s average value:

\[
L \approx 1900 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

- Additional distances similar to Figure 3, unspecified in text. Choose a representative set, e.g.:
  - 500 km,
  - 1000 km,
  - 1900 km,
  - 3000 km,
  - 5000 km.

Expected target:

Using:

\[
\langle \sigma_\phi/S_4 \rangle \approx 5.25 \ \mathrm{rad}
\]

and:

\[
\langle L \rangle \approx 1900 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

should yield:

\[
l_0 \approx 9.7 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

---

# 5.6 Synthetic Experiment C: Error Due to Orientation Error

The paper derives:

\[
\Delta L = 2L\tan(\alpha)\Delta\alpha
\]

where:

- \(\alpha\): scan angle.
- \(\Delta\alpha\): error in irregularity orientation.
- \(L\): true distance from receiver to irregularities.

Use:

\[
\Delta\alpha \approx 0.6^\circ
\]

based on an approximate 1% IGRF magnetic-field model error.

Reproduce Table 1 values:

| \(L\) | \(\alpha=15^\circ\) | \(\alpha=45^\circ\) | \(\alpha=75^\circ\) |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1000 km | 6 km | 21 km | 78 km |
| 3000 km | 17 km | 63 km | 235 km |
| 5000 km | 28 km | 105 km | 392 km |

Implementation test:

1. Simulate observation trajectory with true projection scaling:
   \[
   z = z' \cos\alpha
   \]
2. Apply BP using erroneous angles:
   \[
   \alpha \pm \Delta\alpha
   \]
3. Verify displacement of \(V(L)\) minimum.

---

# 5.7 Synthetic Experiment D: Two Phase Screens, Same Orientation

Purpose:

- Verify that two aligned phase screens are not separately resolvable by BP.

Setup:

- Two phase screens.
- Same orientation.
- Separation distance: 1000 km.
- Same \(\sigma_\phi\) on both screens.

Expected:

- \(V(L)\) does not produce two clearly resolvable minima.
- Locations of both screens cannot be independently recovered.

Second setup:

- Two screens.
- Same orientation.
- One screen has 1.5 times larger \(\sigma_\phi\) than the other.

Expected:

- BP localizes only the screen with larger \(\sigma_\phi\).
- This is true regardless of whether the stronger screen is closer to or farther from the receiver.

---

# 5.8 Synthetic Experiment E: Two Phase Screens, Different Orientation

This requires 3D forward propagation and 2D back propagation.

Use 3D plane-wave propagation:

\[
u(x,y,z)
=
\sum_{mn} c_{mn}
\exp
\left[
ik_{zm}z
+
ik_{yn}y
+
i\sqrt{k^2-k_{zm}^2-k_{yn}^2}(x-x_0)
\right]
\]

Use 2D phase-screen spectrum:

\[
\Phi(k_y,k_z)
\sim
[k_0^2 + (k_y\cos\alpha + k_z\sin\alpha)^2]^\nu
\delta(k_z\cos\alpha - k_y\sin\alpha)
\]

The paper’s parse does not make the exponent fully clear, but this spectrum imposes 1D irregularities with a specified orientation.

Test cases:

1. \(\alpha_1 = 30^\circ\), \(\alpha_2 = 60^\circ\)
2. Swap:
   \[
   \alpha_1 = 60^\circ,\quad \alpha_2 = 30^\circ
   \]

Use equal \(\sigma_\phi\) on both screens.

Expected:

- Only the screen with smaller \(\alpha\) is correctly localized.
- This is true regardless of whether it is closer to or farther from the receiver.

---

# 5.9 Synthetic Experiment F: Thermal Noise

The receiver thermal noise model is additive complex noise:

\[
u = u_1 + iu_2 = A\exp(i\phi)
\]

with noise added independently to \(u_1\) and \(u_2\).

The paper states for open-loop tracking:

\[
\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{A}{\sigma_{u_1}} = \frac{A}{\sigma_{u_2}}
\]

and:

\[
\sigma_\phi = \frac{1}{\mathrm{SNR}}
\]

The reported COSMIC-2 1-Hz SNR statistics:

- Average SNR: approximately 400 V/V.
- More than 90% of geolocations have SNR between 200 and 600 V/V.

For sampling frequency \(f_s\), the effective SNR is reduced by:

\[
\sqrt{f_s}
\]

Example from paper:

- 1-Hz SNR = 400 V/V.
- \(f_s=100\) Hz.
- Effective SNR = 40.
- Thermal phase noise:
  \[
  \sigma_\phi = 1/40 = 0.025 \ \mathrm{rad}
  \]

Model setup:

- Phase screen at:
  \[
  L = 2000 \ \mathrm{km}
  \]
- Use \(\sigma_\phi=1.65\) rad and \(\sigma_\phi=0.25\) rad.
- Test SNR values:
  - 200 V/V,
  - 400 V/V,
  - 600 V/V,
  - possibly noiseless baseline.

Paper-specific spatial sampling:

- Assume:
  \[
  dz/dt = 4 \ \mathrm{km/s}
  \]
- \(f_s = 100\) Hz gives:
  \[
  \Delta z = 40 \ \mathrm{m}
  \]
- The paper uses:
  - 40 km interval in \(z\),
  - windowed to approximately 35 km to remove end effects,
  - 10 m internal grid steps,
  - noise standard deviation increased by factor 2 because of the 10 m modeling grid versus 40 m observational spacing.

Expected:

- Noise shifts the entire \(V(L)\) function upward.
- The shape of \(V(L)\) remains approximately unchanged.
- For \(\sigma_\phi=1.65\), minimum remains detectable.
- For \(\sigma_\phi=0.25\), minimum is marginal or undetectable at low SNR.

---

## 6. Real Observational Back Propagation

The real-data BP differs from synthetic modeling because:

1. Receiver trajectory is not a straight line perpendicular to propagation.
2. GNSS transmitter moves.
3. Incident wave is spherical, not planar.
4. Magnetic-field orientation changes along LOS.

---

# 6.1 Coordinate System for Each 10-s Interval

For each accepted 10-s interval:

1. Select midpoint time \(t_0\).
2. Get midpoint transmitter position:
   \[
   \mathbf{R}_{tx,0}
   \]
3. Get midpoint receiver position:
   \[
   \mathbf{R}_{rx,0}
   \]
4. Define LOS direction.

The paper’s final geolocation uses:

\[
\mathbf{n}_{rt}
=
\frac{\mathbf{R}_{rx} - \mathbf{R}_{tx}}
{|\mathbf{R}_{rx} - \mathbf{R}_{tx}|}
\]

and:

\[
\mathbf{R}_{geo}
=
\mathbf{R}_{rx}
+
L\mathbf{n}_{rt}
\]

Note: depending on sign convention, this vector should point from receiver toward transmitter. Verify sign carefully during implementation.

---

# 6.2 Candidate Magnetic-Field Distance Loop

Outer loop:

\[
L_{\mathrm{mf}} = 100, 200, \ldots, 6000 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

For each \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\):

1. Compute candidate point along LOS:
   \[
   \mathbf{R}_{cand}
   =
   \mathbf{R}_{rx,0}
   +
   L_{\mathrm{mf}}\mathbf{n}_{rt}
   \]
2. Convert to geodetic latitude, longitude, altitude.
3. Evaluate IGRF-13 magnetic-field vector:
   \[
   \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{R}_{cand}, t_0)
   \]
4. Use the direction of \(\mathbf{H}\), not its magnitude.
5. Define the phase-screen plane perpendicular to LOS.
6. Project \(\mathbf{H}\) onto that phase-screen plane.
7. Define the irregularity orientation direction from this projection.
8. Define the BP plane perpendicular to this projected irregularity direction.

---

# 6.3 Scan Angle \(\alpha\)

The scan angle \(\alpha\) is the angle between:

- the LOS scan trajectory on the phase screen, and
- the BP plane.

The paper uses \(\cos\alpha\) in the QC criterion:

\[
\cos\alpha > 0.1
\]

Interpretation:

- If \(\alpha\) is close to \(90^\circ\), then \(\cos\alpha \approx 0\).
- In this geometry, LOS scans along irregularity direction and induces little useful scintillation along the receiver trajectory.
- Therefore reject small \(\cos\alpha\).

Implementation requirement:

- For every \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\), compute \(\cos\alpha(L_{\mathrm{mf}})\).
- Store it for diagnostics and multi-valued-geolocation analysis.

The paper also discusses angle \(\beta\), the angle between the magnetic field and the projected irregularity direction. If \(\beta\) is close to \(90^\circ\), reduction to 2D becomes invalid. However, the final QC list only explicitly uses \(\cos\alpha>0.1\), not a \(\beta\)-threshold.

---

# 6.4 Projection of Observed Signal into BP Plane

For the current \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\)-defined BP plane:

1. Project transmitter and receiver positions at each high-rate sample into the 2D BP coordinate system.
2. Define:
   - \(x\): along midpoint LOS.
   - \(z\): direction in BP plane perpendicular to \(x\).
3. Express sample-wise positions as:
   - transmitter: \((x_1, z_1)\)
   - receiver: \((x_2, z_2)\)

The paper uses the phase-screen location as \(x=0\) in the local coordinate system.

---

# 6.5 Stationary-Transmitter Correction

Because GNSS transmitters move during the 10-s interval, the observed wavefield is not stationary. The paper corrects transmitter and receiver positions using a straight-line-ray approximation.

For a given candidate BP distance \(L\), place the phase screen at:

\[
x = 0
\]

At each sample:

- Actual transmitter:
  \[
  (x_1, z_1)
  \]
- Actual receiver:
  \[
  (x_2, z_2)
  \]

The LOS crosses the phase screen at height:

\[
z_s =
z_1 -
x_1
\frac{z_2-z_1}{x_2-x_1}
\]

The stationary transmitter is fixed at the midpoint coordinate:

\[
(x_{10}, 0)
\]

The corrected receiver coordinates become:

\[
z_2' = z_s \left(1 - \frac{x_2'}{x_{10}}\right)
\]

The paper describes two options:

### Option 1

\[
x_2' = x_2
\]

- Better for diffraction-integral BP.
- Minimizes difference in diffraction effects between original and corrected trajectories.

### Option 2

\[
x_2' = x_{20}
\]

- Receiver is placed on a trajectory perpendicular to propagation direction.
- After interpolation onto uniform \(z_2'\), this is suitable for FFT/plane-wave BP.

The paper does not state unambiguously which option was used in the final CDAAC processing. A robust reproduction should implement both options and compare.

---

# 6.6 Phase Correction

Observed excess phase:

\[
S_{\mathrm{exc}} = S - L_{\mathrm{tr}}
\]

where:

\[
L_{\mathrm{tr}}
=
\sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}
\]

Corrected path length:

\[
L'_{\mathrm{tr}}
=
\sqrt{(x_2'-x_{10})^2 + z_2'^2}
\]

Corrected phase/path quantity:

\[
S' = S_{\mathrm{exc}} + L'_{\mathrm{tr}}
\]

Amplitude is not corrected:

\[
A' = A
\]

Construct corrected complex signal:

\[
u'(z_2') = A'\exp(ikS')
\]

or, if working directly in radians:

\[
u'(z_2') = A'\exp(i\phi')
\]

where \(\phi'=kS'\).

---

# 6.7 Wavefront Curvature Correction

Projection into the BP plane assumes a plane incident wave. The actual wave is spherical with curvature radius approximately equal to transmitter-receiver distance \(R\).

Projection changes curvature radius from:

\[
R
\]

to:

\[
R_1 = R\cos^2\alpha
\]

To restore the curvature, add the quadratic phase/path correction:

\[
\Delta S_{\mathrm{curv}}
=
\frac{z^2\tan^2\alpha}{2R}
\]

to the phase path before BP.

In radians:

\[
\Delta \phi_{\mathrm{curv}}
=
k\frac{z^2\tan^2\alpha}{2R}
\]

---

# 6.8 Back Propagation for Real Data

For each \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\), perform inner-loop BP over distances:

\[
L = 100, 200, \ldots, 6000 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

with step:

\[
\Delta L = 100 \ \mathrm{km}
\]

At each \(L\):

1. Correct transmitter/receiver geometry for a phase screen at that distance.
2. Correct phase.
3. Apply wavefront-curvature correction.
4. Interpolate corrected complex signal onto a uniform \(z\) grid if using FFT plane-wave BP.
5. Back-propagate from corrected receiver trajectory to candidate screen.
6. Compute amplitude:
   \[
   A_{\mathrm{BP}}(z,L) = |u_{\mathrm{BP}}(z,L)|
   \]
7. Compute:
   \[
   V(L) = \frac{\langle A_{\mathrm{BP}}^2\rangle}{\langle A_{\mathrm{BP}}\rangle^2} - 1
   \]

Important:

- A consistent spatial window should be used across \(L\).
- Edge effects should be minimized.
- The paper smooths \(V(L)\), not necessarily the complex field.

---

## 7. Finding the Back-Propagation Minimum

For a fixed \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\), the inner loop yields \(V(L)\).

### 7.1 Smooth \(V(L)\)

The paper applies:

- second-order sliding polynomial regression,
- 1000 km window.

Purpose:

- reduce false rejection due to noise,
- increase robustness of \(Q\),
- preserve \(L_0\) and \(V_0\) approximately.

Implementation:

1. Given discrete \(V(L)\) at 100 km spacing.
2. Use a moving 1000 km window, i.e. approximately 11 points.
3. Fit a quadratic polynomial locally.
4. Evaluate at the central distance.
5. Use edge-handling carefully.

The paper compares raw and smoothed \(V(L)\), then uses the smoothed curve for QC depth estimation.

### 7.2 Locate Global Local Minimum

Find the global local minimum:

\[
V_0 = V(L_0)
\]

Requirements from paper:

- Minimum must be local, not at an end of the BP interval.
- Minimum must not be too close to the ends.
- Specifically, distances \(L_1\) and \(L_2\), defined below, must not be smaller than two 100-km samples.

### 7.3 Define \(L_1\), \(L_2\), \(V_1\), \(V_2\)

Starting from \(L_0\):

- Move toward smaller \(L\) until monotonic increase of \(V(L)\) ends.
- Distance from \(L_0\) to that point is \(L_1\).
- Move toward larger \(L\) until monotonic increase of \(V(L)\) ends.
- Distance from \(L_0\) to that point is \(L_2\).

Then:

\[
V_1 = V(L_0 - L_1)
\]

\[
V_2 = V(L_0 + L_2)
\]

### 7.4 Define Minimum-Depth Metric \(Q\)

\[
Q =
\frac{\min(V_1,V_2)}{V_0}
\]

QC criterion:

\[
Q > 1.2
\]

The paper notes this threshold is partly ad hoc but motivated by noise modeling.

---

## 8. Finding the Final Geolocation

After completing the inner loop for each \(L_{\mathrm{mf}}\), store:

\[
D(L_{\mathrm{mf}}) = L_0 - L_{\mathrm{mf}}
\]

Then search for zero crossings of \(D\).

### 8.1 Zero-Crossing Requirement

A geolocation occurs where:

\[
D(L) = 0
\]

The paper requires:

- \(D(L)\) remains positive on one side and negative on the other side for at least two 100-km steps.
- If crossing is steep and missed between samples, interpolation may be needed, but the paper states crossing may occur at one or several samples or no sample.
- The implementation should search sign changes and exact zero samples.

### 8.2 Estimate Slope \(M\)

For accepted crossings, compute:

\[
M = \left|\frac{\Delta D}{\Delta L}\right|
\]

using the first samples where \(D>0\) and \(D<0\) around the crossing.

The paper suggests \(M\) may be used as an additional uncertainty measure.

### 8.3 Multi-Valued Geolocations

If more than one valid zero crossing occurs in the full 100–6000 km interval, mark the 10-s interval as multi-valued.

The paper states:

- Approximately 8% of COSMIC-2 cases are multi-valued.
- Multi-valued geolocations are discarded.
- They are not interpreted as multiple physical irregularity regions.
- They are caused by the interaction between receiver trajectory direction and changing magnetic-field/scan-angle geometry along LOS.

QC:

- Keep only single-valued geolocations.
- Discard all multi-valued cases.

### 8.4 Final Geolocation Vector

Once the accepted distance \(L\) is obtained:

\[
\mathbf{R}_{geo}
=
\mathbf{R}_{rx}
+
L\mathbf{n}_{rt}
\]

where:

\[
\mathbf{n}_{rt}
=
\frac{\mathbf{R}_{rx}-\mathbf{R}_{tx}}
{|\mathbf{R}_{rx}-\mathbf{R}_{tx}|}
\]

Convert \(\mathbf{R}_{geo}\) to:

- geodetic latitude,
- longitude,
- altitude above reference ellipsoid.

Check sign convention carefully using known geometry. The formula in the paper may depend on vector orientation naming; the result should lie between receiver and transmitter along the LOS.

---

## 9. Final Quality Control Criteria

For a 10-s interval to produce a successful geolocation, require all of the following.

### 9.1 Before BP

1. Phase scintillation:
   \[
   \sigma_\phi > 0.25 \ \mathrm{rad}
   \]

2. Tangent point height:
   \[
   h_{\mathrm{tan}} > 150 \ \mathrm{km}
   \]

### 9.2 During BP

3. BP range:
   \[
   100 \ \mathrm{km} < L < 6000 \ \mathrm{km}
   \]

4. Distance step:
   \[
   \Delta L = 100 \ \mathrm{km}
   \]

5. Valid local minimum of \(V(L)\):
   - not at interval edge,
   - \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) at least two samples, i.e. at least 200 km.

### 9.3 After BP

6. Single-valued geolocation only.

7. Minimum-depth criterion:
   \[
   Q > 1.2
   \]

8. Scan-angle criterion:
   \[
   \cos\alpha > 0.1
   \]

The paper does not include a final explicit altitude filter for the geolocated point, but because the study focuses on the equatorial F layer, altitude distributions should be examined and outliers flagged.

## 12. Suggested Implementation Architecture

Although no code is requested yet, the codebase should be modular.

### 12.1 Data Modules

1. `cosmic_reader`
   - Load HR phase/SNR.
   - Load metadata.
   - Segment into 10-s intervals.

2. `orbit_reader`
   - Load receiver orbits.
   - Interpolate receiver state to HR timestamps.
   - Load/interpolate GNSS transmitter orbits.

3. `igrf_interface`
   - Evaluate IGRF-13 at candidate geolocations and times.

4. `f107_reader`
   - Download/read F10.7 data.

### 12.2 Geometry Modules

1. `los_geometry`
   - Compute LOS vectors.
   - Compute tangent point height.
   - Compute candidate points along LOS.

2. `bp_plane`
   - Define local \(x,z\) coordinates.
   - Project magnetic field.
   - Compute scan angle \(\alpha\).
   - Project receiver/transmitter trajectories.

3. `stationary_correction`
   - Apply moving-transmitter correction.
   - Implement Option 1 and Option 2.

4. `curvature_correction`
   - Add quadratic phase/path correction.

### 12.3 Signal Modules

1. `scintillation_indices`
   - Compute detrended phase.
   - Compute \(\sigma_\phi\).
   - Compute \(S_4\).

2. `complex_signal`
   - Convert amplitude and phase to complex signal.
   - Normalize amplitude if needed.

3. `detrending`
   - Linear/quadratic/high-pass detrending alternatives.

### 12.4 Propagation Modules

1. `fft_propagator_2d`
   - Plane-wave forward/back propagation.
   - Used for synthetic modeling.
   - Possibly used for real BP with corrected uniform receiver trajectory.

2. `kirchhoff_propagator`
   - Diffraction integral for arbitrary trajectories.
   - Important because the paper describes this option, though equation parsing is incomplete.
   - Use for cross-checking.

3. `propagator_3d`
   - 3D forward propagation for misaligned two-screen synthetic tests.

### 12.5 Geolocation Modules

1. `compute_V`
   - Calculate normalized amplitude variance.

2. `smooth_V`
   - Second-order sliding polynomial regression with 1000 km window.

3. `find_local_minimum`
   - Find global local minimum and \(L_0\).

4. `compute_Q`
   - Compute \(L_1,L_2,V_1,V_2,Q\).

5. `find_D_zero_crossings`
   - Determine single or multiple geolocation solutions.

6. `apply_qc`
   - Apply all thresholds.

7. `make_geolocation`
   - Convert distance to lat/lon/alt.

### 12.6 Plotting and Evaluation Modules

1. `plot_synthetic_figures`
2. `plot_bp_diagnostics`
3. `plot_monthly_maps`
4. `plot_l1_l2_comparison`
5. `plot_f107`

## 14. Important Unclear or Ambiguous Details

The following must be resolved or documented during implementation.

### 14.1 Exact Detrending Method

The paper does not specify exactly how phase is detrended before computing \(\sigma_\phi\).

Need to determine from:

- CDAAC documentation,
- related COSMIC-2 scintillation processing papers,
- or sensitivity tests.

### 14.2 Exact Back-Propagation Numerical Method Used Operationally

The paper describes:

1. Kirchhoff diffraction integral for arbitrary trajectories.
2. FFT/plane-wave expansion after correcting to a perpendicular receiver trajectory.

It does not clearly state which was used for the final large-scale processing.

Recommendation:

- Implement FFT/plane-wave BP first because it is computationally efficient and aligns with Option 2.
- Implement Kirchhoff integral later as a validation tool for selected cases.

### 14.3 Kirchhoff Integral Equation Missing in Parse

The parsed paper omits the actual diffraction integral equation.

Need to retrieve from PDF or standard Born & Wolf/Sommerfeld formulation.

### 14.4 Exact Definition of Amplitude

COSMIC-2 amplitude is represented by SNR scaled to 1-Hz band.

Need to determine whether BP uses:

- raw SNR,
- normalized SNR,
- square root of intensity,
- detrended amplitude,
- or amplitude with slow trend removed.

The paper appears to use SNR directly as amplitude, but normalization choices may affect \(V(L)\).

### 14.5 Local Time Reference

For post-sunset filtering, the paper does not explicitly say whether local time is computed at:

- geolocation,
- tangent point,
- receiver,
- or sub-ionospheric point.

Most consistent choice: geolocation longitude.

### 14.6 L1/L2 Test Period

Abstract mentions a 2-month comparison, but body text states February 19 to March 1, 2021.

Use body-text period first, then optionally test a longer two-month interval if needed.

### 14.7 Treatment of GLONASS Open-Loop Signals

The paper states GLONASS needs ground removal of data modulation and phase connection.

If using CDAAC-processed connected phase, this may already be done. Confirm product contents.

### 14.8 Geolocation Vector Sign Convention

The paper’s formula should be checked carefully against actual geometry to ensure \(L\) places the irregularity between receiver and transmitter, not away from transmitter.

Use simple geometric tests.

### Phase 1: Synthetic Propagation Validation

1. Implement 1D random phase screen generator.
2. Implement 2D FFT forward propagation.
3. Implement 2D FFT back propagation.
4. Validate single-screen \(V(L)\) minimum.
5. Reproduce orientation-error relation.
6. Reproduce two-screen aligned experiments.
7. Implement 3D forward propagation for misaligned screens.
8. Implement noise experiments.
9. Implement multi-valued synthetic tests.

2. Parse phase, SNR, sampling rate, signal type.
3. Parse receiver orbit.
4. Load GNSS orbit/clock.
5. Compute \(\sigma_\phi\), \(S_4\), tangent height.
6. Verify against example-like values.

### Phase 3: Real-Geometry BP for Selected Events

3. Implement IGRF-based BP plane.

7. Compare Option 1 vs Option 2 propagation.

1. Implement zero-crossing logic.
2. Implement multi-valued detection.
3. Implement QC thresholds.
4. Generate per-interval geolocation table.
5. Validate accepted/rejected proportions.

### Phase 5: Large-Scale Processing

1. Process full 2021 L1.
2. Process full 2023 L1.
3. Generate monthly 3° × 3° maps.

## 16. Final Reproduction Targets

A successful reproduction should approximately match:

1. Synthetic behavior:
   - \(V(L)\) minimum at phase screen.
   - orientation error scales as \(2L\tan\alpha\Delta\alpha\).
   - two aligned screens not separately resolvable.
   - stronger aligned screen dominates localization.
   - smaller scan-angle misaligned screen dominates localization.
   - noise raises \(V(L)\) baseline.

2. Real COSMIC-2 results:
   - monthly maps showing known seasonal morphology.
   - 2023 substantially more active than 2021.
   - accepted geolocation counts close to:
     - 332k in 2021,
     - 1,108k in 2023.
   - multi-valued cases around 8%.
   - L1/L2 common geolocations around 8k for the specified test period.
   - L1/L2 zonal-difference histogram centered near zero.

3. Diagnostic consistency:
   - average accepted \(\sigma_\phi\) around 1.65 rad.
   - average accepted \(S_4\) around 0.32.
   - average accepted geolocation distance around 1900 km.
   - typical SNR 200–600 V/V.

This plan should provide a strong foundation for implementing the method faithfully while explicitly tracking the paper’s assumptions, numerical choices, QC thresholds, and unresolved ambiguities.

## Logic Analysis: src/experiments/real_data_experiments.py

## Logic Analysis: `src/experiments/real_data_experiments.py`

### 1. Module Purpose

`src/experiments/real_data_experiments.py` implements the paper’s real COSMIC-2 data experiments, not the synthetic propagation validation. It is the orchestration layer for applying the already-implemented geolocation pipeline to real high-rate COSMIC-2 scintillation windows.

This module should contain the `RealDataExperiments` class exactly as specified in the design:

```text
class RealDataExperiments {
    +__init__(config: AppConfig, loader: CosmicLoader, geolocator: Geolocator)
    +process_year(year: int, signal: str) DataFrame
    +process_l1_l2_period(start: date, end: date) DataFrame
    +make_monthly_bins(results: DataFrame) DataFrame
    +pair_l1_l2(l1_results: DataFrame, l2_results: DataFrame) DataFrame
}
```

It must not implement low-level COSMIC-2 parsing, orbit interpolation, phase detrending, back propagation, magnetic-field geometry, or quality control directly. Those responsibilities belong to `CosmicLoader`, `Geolocator`, and the lower-level modules already defined in the design.

Its scientific responsibilities are:

1. Process annual COSMIC-2 high-rate data for a selected signal.
2. Generate accepted geolocation tables for the paper’s monthly L1 map experiment.
3. Filter accepted geolocations to post-sunset local time.
4. Bin accepted geolocations into monthly `3° × 3°` latitude/longitude maps.
5. Process the L1/L2 comparison period.
6. Pair common L1/L2 geolocations.
7. Compute L1–L2 zonal longitude differences in degrees.

All values must come from `config.yaml` through `AppConfig` or from method arguments passed by `main.py` using `config.yaml`.

---

  \]

For GPS L2, the paper excludes L2P because semi-codeless tracking may become unstable under strong scintillation. Therefore:
